none: Passenger automobile average fuel economy, model years 1981-1984, Proposed rulemaking : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration proposed new fuel economy standards for passenger cars for model years 1981-1984. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency to reduce national oil consumption, with standards set to increase from 22 mpg in 1981 to 27 mpg by 1984. This was part of a broader effort to conserve energy amid rising oil imports and costs. The proposal aimed to encourage car manufacturers to adopt new technologies and materials, potentially impacting the environment by reducing gasoline consumption and altering material use in car production.
Passenger automobile average fuel economy, model years 1981-1984, Proposed rulemaking : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration proposed new fuel economy standards for passenger cars for model years 1981-1984. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency to reduce national oil consumption, with standards set to increase from 22 mpg in 1981 to 27 mpg by 1984. This was part of a broader effort to conserve energy amid rising oil imports and costs. The proposal aimed to encourage car manufacturers to adopt new technologies and materials, potentially impacting the environment by reducing gasoline consumption and altering material use in car production.

The proposed rulemaking for passenger automobile fuel economy standards was initiated in response to the energy crisis of the late 1970s, which highlighted the need for improved fuel efficiency to reduce dependence on foreign oil. There was some public support for the initiative, as consumers were increasingly concerned about rising fuel costs and environmental impacts. Ultimately, the standards were implemented, leading to significant advancements in automotive technology and fuel efficiency during the early 1980s.
United States. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration
"The need of the nation to conserve fuel, as viewed by Congress and the President, is the important factor." — Public Interest Campaign
"The AFES schedule which would result in more energy savings should have been investigated also." — Public Interest Campaign
